Iran Nuclear Deal Negotiations — JCPOA Excerpt (Test Fixture)

In July 2015, Iran and the P5+1 nations (United States, United Kingdom, France, Germany, Russia, and China) 
along with the European Union, reached a landmark agreement known as the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action 
(JCPOA) in Vienna. The agreement came after years of intense diplomatic negotiations, beginning in earnest 
after the election of President Hassan Rouhani in 2013.

Under the JCPOA, Iran agreed to significantly limit its uranium enrichment program, reducing its stockpile 
of enriched uranium by 98% and limiting enrichment to 3.67% purity. In exchange, the international 
community agreed to lift comprehensive economic sanctions that had crippled the Iranian economy, including 
oil export restrictions and banking sector sanctions.

The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) was tasked with verifying Iran's compliance through an 
extensive inspection regime, including access to nuclear facilities and continuous monitoring. Iran's 
Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei remained skeptical of Western intentions, while hardliners in Iran's 
Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) opposed any limitations on the nuclear program.

Israel and Saudi Arabia expressed strong opposition to the deal, with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin 
Netanyahu warning that the agreement threatened regional security. The US-Iran trust deficit, built over 
decades of antagonistic relations since the 1979 Islamic Revolution, remained a fundamental obstacle to 
implementation.

In May 2018, President Donald Trump withdrew the United States from the JCPOA, reimposing sweeping 
economic sanctions under a "maximum pressure" campaign. Iran responded by gradually exceeding JCPOA 
limits on enrichment levels and stockpiles. Subsequent Vienna talks in 2021-2022 attempted to restore 
the agreement but failed to reach consensus on sanctions relief and nuclear rollback sequencing.

TODO: This is a test fixture for the extraction pipeline. Real content should be 500+ words.
